title: Conflict Resolution
subtitle: What the Bible Says About Conflict Resolution
summary: Scripture does not promise conflict-free relationships but provides a clear process for addressing disagreements: go directly, speak truthfully, listen humbly, and pursue peace actively.
body:
Jesus provides the most explicit conflict resolution process in Scripture. If a brother sins against you, go and tell him his fault between you and him alone [verse:matt.18.15-17]. The first step is always private, direct conversation — not gossip, not avoidance, not public shaming. Only if private conversation fails does the circle widen.

Paul and Barnabas had a disagreement so sharp that they parted ways [verse:acts.15.39]. Scripture records this without editorial judgment. Not every conflict ends in reconciliation, and sometimes the most productive outcome is an honest parting.

Proverbs offers practical wisdom: a gentle answer turns away wrath, but a harsh word stirs up anger [verse:prov.15.1]. The how of speaking matters as much as the what. James adds that everyone should be quick to listen, slow to speak, and slow to anger [verse:jas.1.19].

Paul instructs the Ephesians to speak the truth in love [verse:eph.4.15]. Truth without love is brutality. Love without truth is sentimentality. Conflict resolution requires both simultaneously.

The goal is always reconciliation where possible. Paul urges: if possible, so far as it depends on you, live peaceably with all [verse:rom.12.18]. The qualifiers are important — 'if possible' and 'so far as it depends on you' — acknowledging that peace sometimes requires the cooperation of both parties.

verses.annotation: Jesus' step-by-step process: private conversation first, then witnesses, then community.
A gentle answer de-escalates — tone shapes outcome.
Paul and Barnabas disagree sharply and part — not all conflicts resolve in unity.
Quick to listen, slow to speak, slow to anger — the order matters.
Speaking truth in love — both elements essential, neither alone sufficient.
Live peaceably with all — with honest qualifiers about what depends on you.
Restore gently, watching yourself — correction with humility.

scholars.quote: Keener explains that Jesus' instruction to go privately first in Matthew 18 was designed to protect the offender's dignity — the escalation process exists precisely because the goal is restoration, not exposure.
scholars.source: A Commentary on the Gospel of Matthew (Keener, 1999)

scholars.quote: Moo reads James 1:19 as a practical antidote to destructive conflict — most relational damage comes from speaking before listening and reacting before understanding.

Schreiner notes that Paul's 'if possible' in Romans 12:18 is a crucial qualifier — it prevents peace from becoming an idol and acknowledges that some situations genuinely cannot be resolved unilaterally.
